segments.text: Hello, everyone. My name is Peter Pras. I'm a developer advocate at Autodesk.
In this lightning talk, I want to do a quick overview and introduction into recommended
auth patterns for custom MCP servers that want to integrate with Autodesk platform services.
Now, there are many different ways in which MCP servers are deployed, hosted, and used.
In our research, we focused on the following narrow scope. We are looking at third party
MCP servers developed by our developer community that want to integrate with different APIs
in our platform, Autodesk platform services. We're looking at servers, MCP servers that are
deployed to the cloud and use the streamable HTTP transport. So no standard IO, no locally
running MCP servers covered here. In this talk, we give a quick refresher and terminology
update for OAuth and MCP specifications as these are crucial to the rest of the content.
Then we present three recommended auth patterns for different types of access scenarios.
And we wrap up with a quick summary and comparison of these methods.
OAuth is a popular auth standard used by many different web services and cloud providers,
including Autodesk platform services. And what's important for our lightning talk today
is the terminology. Let's start with roles. And the OAuth standard defines a role of a client.
A client is an application that wants to access protected resource on behalf of a specific resource
owner. In the context of APS, a client could be your web application or desktop application
with its own client ID or client secret that you create on the developer board.
The other role resource owner is an entity that is capable of granting access to a protected
resource. In context of APS, this would be the end user logging into your APS application,
having access to their existing design data in Autodesk Forma or Fusion Teams, for example.
Authorization server is an entity, a role that is capable of
validating, authenticating users and generating access tokens that then provide access to
different types of resources on behalf of the users. In case of Autodesk platform services,
this would be our authentication server and the authentication API. And finally, the role of
a resource server, this is the entity that posts the protected data that OAuth clients can
access with the proper access tokens. In case of APS, this would be the model derivative API.
For example, that you can access with an access token that's been either generated for your
application or for your user level identity. And another important part are the OAuth blows.
In our platform, we recognize mainly the following two, the client credentials flow,
also called two-legged OAuth flow. This is where the trust is established between your application,
the OAuth clients, and the authorization server. This is the so-called app-level identity.
The other one called authorization code flow, also known as three-legged OAuth flow,
is where there is a third party also involved in this process of establishing trust. And that is
your user. So in this case, when using the authorization code flow, your application receives
access tokens that actually represent a specific user. And these access tokens can then be used to
access three-legged APIs in our platform. Now moving on to the MCP specification, focusing just on
the auth part. The authentication in MCP is based on the OAuth standard version 2.1.
What's important here is that an MCP server is considered an OAuth resource server. So going
back to the left side, that would be equal to model derivative API, for example. And an MCP
client, which could be cloud, chat, GPT, or VS Code GitHub co-pilot, in this case, according to
the MCP specification, is considered an OAuth client. So that would be similar to your web application,
or any kind of application that you create on the Autodesk platform services developer portal.
What's also important to mention is that the authorization, according to the MCP spec,
is optional. But when it's used for HTTP-based transport, such as the streamable HTTP,
it should, the implementation should conform to the spec. And on the other side, when using
standard IOT transport and locally running MCP servers, the authorization, the implementation
should not follow the auth spec. And instead, it should just get whatever auth credentials from
the environment in which the local MCP server is running. Now, the reason we say this is that
the combination of the situation on the OAuth side and the requirements from the MCP spec
introduce an important challenge. We call it a two-layer auth challenge. Looking back on the OAuth
side, when you think about this, the custom MCP server that you're building has two roles. It needs to
act as a resource server to the MCP clients. So when somebody like Claude or Chid GPD wants to
connect to your MCP server, that client first needs to ask the user to grant access to log in with
a specific auth server and grant access to some resources. And only then with access tokens,
they should be talking to your custom MCP server. So in this case, the layer one, your server acts as
an OAuth resource server, again, like model derivative API. But then, if your custom server also
wants to call Autodesk platform services and access our APIs, the same MCP server also needs to act
as an OAuth client accessing resource servers such as the model derivative API or Autodesk
API. That's an important challenge to keep in mind. And what makes things even worse in a way is that
according to the MCP specification, it is explicitly forbidden to pass access tokens that your
server receives from the clients, such as Claude or Chid GPD, and reuse the same tokens to call
upstream APIs, such as model derivative for Autodesk for my APIs. This is strictly forbidden by
the MCP spec as it can introduce vulnerability, such as confused deputy security vulnerability that
you can read more about in the linked sections of the MCP spec here. So with that said, what options do
we have? As I said, we are covering three, we're recommending auth patterns for three very common
use cases and usage scenarios. First one is what we call the 2LO, the 2-legged OAuth pattern.
This is where you're building an MCP server that really only needs to access two-legged APIs in
our platform, such as the object, storage service, model derivative for maybe design automation API.
In this case, your MCP server doesn't really have to require authentication from the client.
In this case, although it is recommended that you still do provide an additional auth layer
between the MCP client and your MCP server. But then looking at our sequence diagram here,
again, whether or not you establish some auth boundaries between the MCP client and your server
is up to you. But then when your server wants to call our 2-legged APIs, you can simply ask our
auth server for a 2-legged token and use the token to call APIs directly. This is a simple approach.
Again, useful for when you're running backgrounds, maybe when you're developing MCP servers that
want to be accessed in the background without any user being involved. The implementation is
very simple. No user interaction is needed. Now, the limitation, of course, as I said, is that
this approach only works for auth platform services APIs that support 2-legged tokens, such as
modularivative or automation API. Also, it's important to mention that 2-legged authentication
is not recommended for new integrations as these are considered less secure in general. So,
in the future, we may be looking at replacing or enhancing the 2-legged auth with more secure options.
Also, one note I wanted to add to this slide right before the recording is there has been some
news in the MCP spec where there are now some official extensions to the MCP specification.
And one of them is actually focused on authentication. And the MCP extension now supports clan
credentials flow, the 2-legged, as we call it, natively as part of the spec. So, if this is
something you would be interested in, I definitely recommend that you take a look at the new MCP auth
extensions. The second pattern, what we call the SSA pattern, is similar to 2-legged,
but in this case, it's using secure service account API. So, if you're not familiar, secure service
accounts are a relatively new feature in our platform that lets you access relegged APIs
in a background automated type of way with no user presence. So, you can create a sort of a robot
account which acts as an actual physical user. You can generate, and your MCP server can then generate
access tokens relegged, representing that specific robot user and call three-legged APIs in our
platform. And then, of course, for that, you will need to invite this robot user with its own
automatically generated email address into your project in Autodesk 4-mile or Fusion teams,
as needed. So, it gives you more granular access control as well. Now, for MCP servers, this is
something we have some examples for this as well, where, again, for the authentication between MCP
clients and your server, you can either skip the auth completely or you can introduce a third-party
auth indication using other identity providers if needed. And after that, what happens is
when a client, an MCP client calls a tool in your MCP server, you, again, without any user involved,
you can simply talk to our authentication server, generate an assertion, which is based on a
private key that we generate for your robot account, the secure service account. You can exchange
that for a three-legged access token which captures the identity of that robot account. And you can
then use that token to call APIs in our platform that require user context, such as
AEC data model API. So, again, very simple, very similar to the tool-legged, but in this case,
also giving you access to more APIs that do require a user context.
All right. Now, also, one downside to mention here is you need to be very careful. This depends on
the type of how you would deploy this kind of MCP server, but one thing to keep in mind is that
if your MCP server uses a single robot account to access three-legged APIs, any end user that
connects to your MCP server will get access to the same data, right? End users will not be able to
use the MCP server to access their own Autodesk format or fusion team designs, but only those that
are accessible to this one single configured robot account, the secure service account.
And finally, the three-legged pattern. This is the one that we recommend for most use cases,
as we believe it's going to be the one that's most widely used and useful. Plus, it's also
following the requirements of the MCP spec to the letter. And so this is the three-legged pattern
based on something that's called URL elicitation in the MCP spec. So the MCP spec talks about a feature
called URL elicitation, which is basically a way for an MCP server to ask a user to open a link.
And in that part of the specification, you can actually read more details there and find out
that the MCP spec recommends this URL elicitation for third-party logins.
Let me take you through the sequence diagram and explain how this works.
So in this case, an end user may open Cloud or Chat GPT and connect to your MCP server and say,
I want to list my projects in Autodesk format. And this case, what you can do is,
on your MCP server site, you can check if you already have some credentials, some access token
available for a specific user. And if you don't, you can immediately respond from your MCP server saying,
before doing this, before viewing your projects, you need to log in first. Please follow this link.
This is where your MCP server can act, just like any regular APS web application.
That's asking users to log in. So you provide an authorization URL in the response from the tool call.
And then this link will be presented directly to the user. The user can open the link
in the browser and go through, again, the exact same login process as if your MCP server was just a
regular web application. They're presented with a login form. After that, they're presented with
the consent form where they can grant access to their data based on your scopes. And after that,
your MCP server receives a temporary code and can exchange it for an actual access token and
refresh token. Now, the important part is that, in this case, it is the responsibility of your MCP
server to cache and manage these credentials per user session. Now, luckily, when MCP clients connect
to your server, your server can create something called an MCP session ID. And that could be a way
for your MCP server to actually cache, save and manage and cache access tokens for individual
user sessions. So after that, when the user has logged in, they can retry the tool call,
let's say, to receive their list of projects from Bormar Fusion. And in that case, your MCP server,
based on their session ID already finds an access token and can use it to call APIs on our side
in our platform such as Forma or AEC data model. Okay. This is the process for the three-legged
the three-legged API access. So now, again, the benefits of this approach is this is completely
valid and it satisfies the restrictions imposed by the MCP specification. And it does not require
any special capabilities on the MCP client side. However, on the other hand, it's important to
keep in mind that it requires your MCP server to be stateful. In that case, your MCP server needs to
manage the credentials of individual users that are connected to it through different MCP clients
and cache them and manage them over its lifecycle. And also, what's important to mention here is that
of course, since your MCP server is now cache and credentials based on some kind of an MCP session ID,
this could potentially be a security concern as some bad actors could try and guess session IDs
and try and act as different users by guessing their MCP session ID. This, however, can be mitigated
in different ways as well. And we will have some additional resources coming up
expanding on this. We also are planning an AU class that will go into more detail on how you can
actually mitigate this one minor security concern. You could do this by, for example, rotating,
rotating the session IDs on your MCP server or use other cryptographic methods to actually generate
IDs that would be harder to guess by bad actors. All right, so a quick summary. So for the
authentication between MCP clients and your server for the first two-legged pattern and the SSA,
secure service, a competitor, it is really up to you. You can either skip that auth completely
or you can use a third party, for example, your own enterprise login if you need to.
For the three-legged, you could also skip the auth between client and server here, but we still
recommend that you introduce and at least maybe a simple API key or a simple protection for
the first layer of this architecture. For the auth between your server and on-desperate from
services, you can either use the client credentials, the tool-legged, or secure service accounts if you
need access to our APIs that do require a user context. And in case of the last pattern, this
would be the typical, just like a regular APS web application workflow, the login dance where
you open your ask a user to click a link that will take them to the Autodesk login, where they can
grant access to their data and generate a temporary code for your MCP server, which will then
exchange it for a regular access token. Now, whose data are you actually accessing with these
patterns or the two-legged, this is the app level identity, right? So there is no user per say.
Whatever data that MCP server would have access to would be the data that your application itself,
the client ID, has access to. For the SSA pattern, that would be the service account, the robot account,
so whatever content you give the robot access, the robot account access to in Autodesk Formar
Fusion teams, that's what the MCP server would have access to. And in case of the three-legged,
of course, this is the ideal what we believe that most of our customers will be looking for.
This is where users who use your MCP server in Clawed or CHGPD will actually be able to see their
own data. All of these patterns are available and supported today. And as we mentioned, again,
the two-legged would be most useful for accessing two-legged APIs such as OSS, automation,
or model derivative API. The SSA pattern would be useful for when building MCP servers that will be
by agents without any user actually sitting in front of the browser. So for automated backgrounds,
workflows. And finally, again, the three-legged pattern would be most useful for MCP servers that
would be actively used by agents with end-user sitting in front of an application like Clawed or
CHGPD trying to access their data. So with user present. And finally, we also have a simple code sample
on our official GitHub org implemented in Python where you can find all these three patterns
implemented for your reference. And we also have a Wiki page on that same GitHub repo that goes
into more detail of the pros and the downsize and some mitigations of potential security concerns
for the last three-legged pattern. And with that, that's all from my side. Thank you for your
attention and good luck with building your MCP servers.
